clean_story: Body’s hidden blueprint.  X-rays sliced into layers. "CT scan" stands for computed tomography—a way to take hundreds of X-ray slices and stack them like a loaf of bread, revealing what’s inside without cutting. Imagine flipping through a deck of cards where each card shows a thinner slice of an apple—core, seeds, skin—until the whole fruit appears in your mind. That’s how radiologists "see" tumors, fractures, or clogged arteries. Here’s the mechanism:You lie still as a doughnut-shaped machine spins around you, firing X-rays from 360 degrees. Detectors on the opposite side catch the beams, weaker where dense tissue—like bone—blocks them. A computer crunches the differences, assigning each slice a grayscale value: white for bone, black for air, gray for soft tissue. Example:A 5-second brain CT can spot a hemorrhage faster than an MRI, while a chest CT pinpoints a 2mm lung nodule invisible on standard X-rays. Precision matters—modern scanners resolve details thinner than a credit card. Three related subjects are medical imaging, radiation physics, and MRI contrasts.
